items.myth: A perceptron can learn any classification problem if you train long enough.
items.reality: It learns only linearly separable functions. Non-separable data (XOR, heavy overlap) has no single hyperplane solution — training error cannot reach zero regardless of epochs.
items.example: XOR with a single Perceptron(max_iter=10000) still misclassifies one corner; accuracy caps at 75%.

items.myth: The perceptron and logistic regression are completely different models.
items.reality: Both use linear score z = wᵀx + b. They differ in activation (step vs sigmoid) and loss (mistake-driven vs BCE), not in the linear boundary family.
items.example: On separable 2D blobs, both can produce similar decision lines but logistic regression also outputs P(y=1|x) near the boundary.

items.myth: Weights only update when the prediction is correct.
items.reality: The perceptron learning rule updates on misclassification only — correct predictions skip the update step.
items.example: If ŷ = y for an example, w and b stay unchanged for that step; wrong side of the boundary triggers w ← w + η(y − ŷ)x.

items.myth: Step activation and ReLU are the same thing.
items.reality: Step outputs a binary 0/1 for classification at one threshold. ReLU outputs max(0, z) as a continuous activation feeding hidden layers — differentiable almost everywhere and used in deep nets, not as a final hard class label in the same way.
items.example: z = −0.3 → step gives 0; ReLU gives 0. z = 2.5 → step gives 1; ReLU gives 2.5 (preserves magnitude).
